items.prompt_md: While binary searching Koko's eating speed, `hours_needed(mid) <= h` comes back true. What should happen next?
options: `hi = mid` — mid already works, so a slower speed might also work; keep mid in range and keep searching left
`lo = mid + 1` — mid is too slow, try a faster speed
Return `mid` immediately as the final answer
Restart the search with `lo = 1`
items.answer: `hi = mid` — mid already works, so a slower speed might also work; keep mid in range and keep searching left
items.explanation_md: Since mid is feasible, the minimum working speed is at most mid, so `hi` shrinks down to mid instead of jumping past it — we're hunting the smallest speed that still works.

items.prompt_md: What does it mean for a feasibility check to be "monotonic" in an answer-space binary search?
options: Once a candidate value passes the check, every larger candidate also passes (or, for a maximum, every smaller one also passes) — the yes/no answers never flip back and forth
The check must return the same true/false answer for every candidate value
The candidate values must be sorted numerically before checking them
The check must run in constant time regardless of the candidate
items.answer: Once a candidate value passes the check, every larger candidate also passes (or, for a maximum, every smaller one also passes) — the yes/no answers never flip back and forth
items.explanation_md: Monotonicity is what turns a pile of candidate values into a clean sorted boundary between "no" and "yes", even though nothing about the candidates themselves is stored in a sorted array.

items.prompt_md: Why does Koko's search initialize `lo, hi = 1, max(piles)` instead of, say, `0, len(piles)`?
options: Because `lo` and `hi` must bound the range of *candidate answers* (possible eating speeds), and the slowest useful speed is 1 while the fastest ever needed is eating the biggest pile in one hour
Because the array must always be indexed starting from 1
Because `len(piles)` is always too small to be a valid speed
Because binary search always starts its range at the array's own length
items.answer: Because `lo` and `hi` must bound the range of *candidate answers* (possible eating speeds), and the slowest useful speed is 1 while the fastest ever needed is eating the biggest pile in one hour
items.explanation_md: Here the binary search isn't over array indices at all — `lo`/`hi` bound the space of possible speeds, and no speed faster than the largest single pile is ever necessary.

items.prompt_md: Why does the answer-space search use `while lo < hi` (not `lo <= hi`) together with `hi = mid` instead of `hi = mid - 1`?
options: Because `mid` might itself be the answer, and this loop shape is designed so `lo` and `hi` converge exactly onto that smallest feasible value rather than skipping past it
Because `lo < hi` is required whenever the range contains only integers
Because it makes the loop run in constant time
Because `hi = mid - 1` would always cause an infinite loop
items.answer: Because `mid` might itself be the answer, and this loop shape is designed so `lo` and `hi` converge exactly onto that smallest feasible value rather than skipping past it
items.explanation_md: Since `mid` is a candidate that could be the final answer when it's feasible, `hi` must be allowed to land exactly on `mid` rather than jump past it — that's why this boundary-search shape pairs `lo < hi` with `hi = mid`.

items.prompt_md: Once the loop `while lo < hi` ends, why is `lo` (which now equals `hi`) guaranteed to be the minimum feasible speed?
options: Because the loop invariant keeps the true answer inside `[lo, hi]` at every step, and the range only closes down to a single value once every infeasible candidate below it has been excluded
Because `lo` is always incremented exactly once per hour needed
Because `hi` always starts out equal to the correct answer
Because `mid` is recomputed to equal `lo` on the final iteration by coincidence
items.answer: Because the loop invariant keeps the true answer inside `[lo, hi]` at every step, and the range only closes down to a single value once every infeasible candidate below it has been excluded
items.explanation_md: Every iteration either confirms mid works and pulls `hi` down to it, or confirms mid fails and pushes `lo` past it — so the surviving single value is the smallest one that passed.

items.prompt_md: What is the time complexity of the `hours_needed(k)` feasibility check itself, for `n` piles?
options: O(n)
O(log n)
O(n log n)
items.answer: O(n)
items.explanation_md: Checking feasibility for one candidate speed means summing one ceiling-division term per pile — a single linear pass over the n piles.

items.prompt_md: With n piles and a candidate range of size m = max(piles), what is the overall time complexity of binary searching the answer space?
options: O(n log m)
O(n)
O(n * m)
O(log m)
items.answer: O(n log m)
items.explanation_md: The range of candidate speeds shrinks in O(log m) steps, and each step's feasibility check costs O(n), giving O(n log m) overall.

items.prompt_md: What would the time complexity be if you tried every candidate speed one at a time, from 1 upward, instead of binary searching?
options: O(n * m)
O(n log m)
O(log m)
O(n)
items.answer: O(n * m)
items.explanation_md: Trying every speed from 1 to m and re-running the O(n) feasibility check each time costs O(n) per candidate across up to m candidates — O(n * m) in the worst case, versus O(n log m) for the binary-searched version.

items.prompt_md: Suppose a feasibility check were NOT monotonic — some small values pass, then some fail, then some pass again. What breaks?
options: Binary search can no longer be trusted to find the true minimum feasible value, since discarding a half based on one comparison might throw away the actual answer
Nothing breaks — binary search still finds an answer, just more slowly
The feasibility check would need to run in O(log n) instead of O(n)
The candidate range would need to be sorted first
items.answer: Binary search can no longer be trusted to find the true minimum feasible value, since discarding a half based on one comparison might throw away the actual answer
items.explanation_md: The entire halving strategy relies on "pass/fail flips exactly once." Without that guarantee, a single comparison at mid can't safely rule out an entire half — the real answer could be hiding in the discarded side.

items.prompt_md: A learner writes `hi = mid - 1` instead of `hi = mid` in the feasible branch (`hours_needed(mid) <= h`). What bug does this introduce?
options: It can skip past and permanently exclude `mid` even though `mid` might be the true minimum feasible speed, causing the search to return a value that's too large
It causes an infinite loop that never terminates
It has no effect since `mid` is never a valid answer anyway
It only matters when `h` equals the number of piles
items.answer: It can skip past and permanently exclude `mid` even though `mid` might be the true minimum feasible speed, causing the search to return a value that's too large
items.explanation_md: Because mid passed the feasibility check, it's a legitimate candidate for the final answer. Excluding it with `hi = mid - 1` can throw away the correct minimum and leave the search converging on a larger, suboptimal speed.

items.prompt_md: A learner writes `lo = mid` instead of `lo = mid + 1` in the infeasible branch (`hours_needed(mid) > h`). What's the risk?
options: Since `mid` has already been ruled out as too slow, leaving it inside the range can cause `lo` to stop advancing and the loop to never terminate
It has no effect, since `mid` will just be recomputed to a different value next time regardless
It causes the search to return a speed that's too small
It changes the complexity from O(log m) to O(m)
items.answer: Since `mid` has already been ruled out as too slow, leaving it inside the range can cause `lo` to stop advancing and the loop to never terminate
items.explanation_md: `mid` failed the check, so it can never be the answer — but if `lo` is set back to `mid` instead of `mid + 1`, integer division can recompute the same `mid` forever, exactly the infinite-loop trap that off-by-one bugs cause in ordinary binary search too.

items.prompt_md: If the feasibility check itself cost O(n log n) instead of O(n), what would the overall complexity of the answer-space binary search become (n piles, candidate range size m)?
options: O(n log n log m)
O(n log m)
O(n * m)
O(log m)
items.answer: O(n log n log m)
items.explanation_md: The range still shrinks in O(log m) steps, but now each step's feasibility check costs O(n log n), multiplying out to O(n log n log m) overall.

items.prompt_md: You need the minimum eating speed such that all the piles can be finished within h hours, where finishing-time-at-speed-k only gets shorter as k grows.
options: Try every speed one at a time, from slowest to fastest
Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
Hash map keyed by pile size
Two pointers over the sorted piles
items.answer: Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
items.explanation_md: "Minimum value such that a monotonic condition holds" is the answer-space trigger — feasibility only improves as the candidate speed grows, so it forms a clean boundary to binary search.

items.prompt_md: You need the minimum ship capacity that lets you deliver all packages, in order, within D days, where a larger capacity can only ever finish in the same number of days or fewer.
options: Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
Linear scan through every capacity value
Hash map of package weight to count
Two pointers over the package list
items.answer: Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
items.explanation_md: Capacity feasibility is monotonic — any capacity larger than a working one also works — so the minimum working capacity is found by binary searching the range of capacities.

items.prompt_md: You must find the smallest integer divisor such that dividing every number in an array by it (rounding up) and summing the results stays at or below a given threshold.
options: Two pointers over the array
Hash map of divisor to remainder
Try every divisor one at a time starting from 1
Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
items.answer: Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
items.explanation_md: A larger divisor can only shrink or hold steady the summed result, never increase it — that monotonic feasibility is exactly what lets you binary search for the smallest divisor instead of testing every one.

items.prompt_md: You're given an unsorted array of integers and need the original indices of two numbers whose product equals a given target.
options: Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
Hash map of value to index
Two pointers over the sorted array
Try every candidate value one at a time
items.answer: Hash map of value to index
items.explanation_md: There's no feasibility check or monotonic condition here at all — you need to find an actual complementary value in unsorted data while preserving indices, which is a hashing problem, not a search-range problem.

items.prompt_md: You need the minimum number of days to wait such that at least m bouquets can be made from adjacent, already-bloomed flowers, where waiting longer can only ever bloom the same flowers or more.
options: Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
Hash map of flower index to bloom day
Two pointers scanning the flower array
Try every day one at a time from day 1
items.answer: Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
items.explanation_md: "Can we make m bouquets by day k?" only ever flips from no to yes as k increases, never back — a monotonic feasibility check over candidate days, the exact shape a binary search over the answer space targets.

items.prompt_md: You're scanning an unsorted list of customer IDs and need to know whether any ID shows up more than once.
options: Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
Hash set of seen IDs
Try every candidate threshold one at a time
Two pointers over the ID list
items.answer: Hash set of seen IDs
items.explanation_md: There's no candidate value to search over and no monotonic condition — this is a plain duplicate check, which a seen-set solves directly in one pass.

items.prompt_md: You must find the maximum number of chocolates you can give each of k friends equally, from piles of chocolates, where giving fewer chocolates per friend can only ever stay feasible or become easier, never infeasible.
options: Try every amount one at a time starting from the largest
Hash map of pile size to friend count
Two pointers over the sorted piles
Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
items.answer: Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
items.explanation_md: This is the maximum-feasible-value version of the same monotonic trigger: as the candidate amount shrinks, feasibility can only improve, so the boundary is found by binary searching the answer space rather than checking every amount.

items.prompt_md: You need to group a list of unsorted product names by category label so you can count how many products fall into each category.
options: Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
Two pointers over the product list
Hash map of category to running count
Try every category one at a time
items.answer: Hash map of category to running count
items.explanation_md: Counting occurrences by key has no monotonic feasibility condition and no candidate range to narrow — it's a direct hashing/tallying problem.

items.prompt_md: You must find the minimum possible value for the largest element after splitting an array into k contiguous subarrays, where allowing a larger cap can only ever make splitting easier, never harder.
options: Try every cap value one at a time from the array's max
Two pointers over the array
Hash map of subarray sum to index
Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
items.answer: Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
items.explanation_md: "Can we split into k parts each at most cap?" flips from no to yes exactly once as cap increases — the monotonic feasibility signature that answer-space binary search is built for.

items.prompt_md: You need to place k routers along a line of candidate positions so that the minimum distance between any two routers is as large as possible, where shrinking the required minimum distance can only ever make placement easier.
options: Hash map of position to router count
Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
Try every minimum distance one at a time starting from 0
Two pointers over the candidate positions
items.answer: Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
items.explanation_md: The greedy "can we place k routers with at least this minimum spacing?" check flips monotonically as the spacing shrinks or grows, so the maximum feasible spacing is found by binary searching that answer space.

items.prompt_md: You're given an unsorted list of transaction amounts and must find whether any single amount appears more times than every other amount combined.
options: Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
Try every amount one at a time as a candidate
Two pointers over the transaction list
Hash map counting occurrences of each amount
items.answer: Hash map counting occurrences of each amount
items.explanation_md: This is a frequency question over unsorted data with no monotonic feasibility check to exploit — a straightforward count-and-compare with a hash map, not a binary-searchable boundary.

items.prompt_md: You need the minimum number of hours a single machine must run per day to finish all pending jobs within a deadline, where running more hours per day can only ever finish jobs sooner or on the same day, never later.
options: Hash map of job id to duration
Try every hour value one at a time
Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
Two pointers over the job list
items.answer: Binary-search the answer space using the feasibility check
items.explanation_md: "Finishes in time at this rate?" only ever turns from no to yes as the rate increases, so the smallest sufficient rate is found by binary searching the range of candidate hours instead of testing each one.
